⌂ Hub MATHS-IV · LEARN & PRACTICE
U1U2U3U4U5
BS 401 MT · Semester 4 · a slow, step-by-step guide

Mathematics – IV
learn it, then practise it

The working mathematics of engineering: transforms that turn calculus into algebra, numerical methods that compute answers when formulas run out, and the statistics of fitting models to real data.

How to use this page. Transforms are all about tables: learn the standard transforms cold, and most problems become "look up, rearrange, look back up". Numerical methods reward neat, patient arithmetic — set out your steps in a table.

Unit I

Laplace Transforms

A machine that turns a differential equation into an ordinary algebra problem — solve the algebra, then transform back.

1.1 The big idea1.2 The transform table1.3 Derivatives & shifting1.4 Inverse transform1.5 Solving equations1.6 Practice
Lesson 1.1

The big idea

In plain words

The Laplace transform takes a function of time \(f(t)\) and turns it into a function of a new variable \(s\), written \(F(s)\). The point: hard operations like differentiation become easy algebra in the \(s\)-world. Solve there, then transform back.

The three-step strategy

(1) Transform the whole differential equation into the \(s\)-world. (2) Solve the resulting algebraic equation for \(F(s)\). (3) Transform back ("inverse Laplace") to get \(f(t)\).

Lesson 1.2

The transform table — memorise these

Standard Laplace transforms

\(\mathcal L\{1\}=\dfrac1s\)    \(\mathcal L\{t^n\}=\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}\)    \(\mathcal L\{e^{at}\}=\dfrac{1}{s-a}\)

\(\mathcal L\{\sin at\}=\dfrac{a}{s^2+a^2}\)    \(\mathcal L\{\cos at\}=\dfrac{s}{s^2+a^2}\)

Worked example 1.2a warm-up

Find \(\mathcal L\{3t^2+e^{4t}\}\).

Term by term \(\mathcal L\{t^2\}=\dfrac{2!}{s^3}=\dfrac{2}{s^3}\), so \(\mathcal L\{3t^2\}=\dfrac{6}{s^3}\). And \(\mathcal L\{e^{4t}\}=\dfrac{1}{s-4}\).

Add \(\dfrac{6}{s^3}+\dfrac{1}{s-4}\).

Lesson 1.3

Transform of a derivative, and shifting

Two essential rules

Derivative rule: \(\mathcal L\{f'(t)\}=sF(s)-f(0)\). This is what brings the initial condition into play.

First shifting theorem: \(\mathcal L\{e^{at}f(t)\}=F(s-a)\) — multiply by \(e^{at}\) in time, shift \(s\to s-a\).

Why the derivative rule matters

Each derivative becomes a multiplication by \(s\), minus a starting value. That's precisely how a differential equation collapses into algebra. Forgetting the \(-f(0)\) term is the most common error — it drops the initial condition.

Lesson 1.4

The inverse transform (with partial fractions)

The idea

Going back from \(F(s)\) to \(f(t)\) means recognising \(F(s)\) as one of the standard table shapes. If it doesn't match, break it into simpler pieces with partial fractions first.

Worked example 1.4a partial fractions

Find \(\mathcal L^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s-1)(s-2)}\right\}\).

Partial fractions \(\dfrac{1}{(s-1)(s-2)}=\dfrac{-1}{s-1}+\dfrac{1}{s-2}\).

Invert each piece \(\mathcal L^{-1}\{\tfrac{1}{s-a}\}=e^{at}\). So this is \(-e^{t}+e^{2t}\).

Lesson 1.5

Solving a differential equation with Laplace

Worked example 1.5a full problem

Solve \(y'+y=1\) with \(y(0)=0\).

Transform both sides \(\mathcal L\{y'\}+\mathcal L\{y\}=\mathcal L\{1\}\Rightarrow \big(sY-y(0)\big)+Y=\dfrac1s\).

Insert the initial condition \(y(0)=0\), so \((s+1)Y=\dfrac1s\).

Solve for Y \(Y=\dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}=\dfrac1s-\dfrac{1}{s+1}\) (partial fractions).

Invert \(y(t)=1-e^{-t}\).

Unit I don't-lose-marks list
  • Derivative rule: never drop the \(-f(0)\) term.
  • Do partial fractions before inverting; the table only knows standard shapes.
  • The convolution theorem \(\mathcal L\{f*g\}=F(s)G(s)\) helps invert awkward products.

Practice — Unit I

Keep the transform table beside you; most steps are look-ups.

Q1

Find \(\mathcal L\{5+2t\}\).

SOLUTION \(\mathcal L\{5\}=\dfrac5s\), \(\mathcal L\{2t\}=\dfrac{2}{s^2}\).

\(\dfrac5s+\dfrac{2}{s^2}\).

Q2

Find \(\mathcal L\{\sin 3t\}\).

SOLUTION \(\dfrac{a}{s^2+a^2}\) with \(a=3\).

\(\dfrac{3}{s^2+9}\).

Q3

Find \(\mathcal L^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2+4}\right\}\).

SOLUTION Match \(\dfrac{a}{s^2+a^2}\) with \(a=2\); we have \(\dfrac{1}{s^2+4}=\dfrac12\cdot\dfrac{2}{s^2+4}\).

\(\tfrac12\sin 2t\).

Q4

Solve \(y'+2y=0\), \(y(0)=3\).

SOLUTION \((sY-3)+2Y=0\Rightarrow(s+2)Y=3\Rightarrow Y=\dfrac{3}{s+2}\).

\(y=3e^{-2t}\).

Unit II

Fourier Transforms

Like the Fourier series, but for one-off (non-repeating) signals over the whole line — the basis of frequency analysis.

2.1 Series vs transform2.2 The transforms2.3 Worked examples2.4 Practice
Lesson 2.1

Series versus transform

In plain words

A Fourier series (from Maths III) breaks up a repeating signal into discrete frequencies. A Fourier transform does the same job for a non-repeating signal spread over the whole line — it gives a continuous spread of frequencies.

Lesson 2.2

The Fourier transform and its sine/cosine versions

The definitions

Fourier transform: \(F(s)=\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x)\,e^{isx}\,dx\).

Sine transform: \(F_s(s)=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}f(x)\sin sx\,dx\).   Cosine transform: \(F_c(s)=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}f(x)\cos sx\,dx\).

Use the cosine transform for even functions, the sine transform for odd — matched to the boundary condition you're given.

Lesson 2.3

Worked examples

Worked example 2.3a standard

Find the Fourier cosine transform of \(f(x)=e^{-ax}\) (with \(a>0\)).

Set up \(F_c(s)=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}\cos sx\,dx\).

Use the standard integral \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}\cos sx\,dx=\dfrac{a}{a^2+s^2}\).

Answer \(F_c(s)=\dfrac{a}{a^2+s^2}\).

Worked example 2.3b the sine partner

Find the Fourier sine transform of \(f(x)=e^{-ax}\).

Standard integral \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}\sin sx\,dx=\dfrac{s}{a^2+s^2}\).

Answer \(F_s(s)=\dfrac{s}{a^2+s^2}\).

Unit II don't-lose-marks list
  • Textbooks differ by a constant (the \(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\) factor) — use your course's convention consistently.
  • Choose sine vs cosine transform based on the given condition, not by guessing.

Practice — Unit II

The two standard exponential integrals above cover most exam questions.

Q1

Find the cosine transform of \(e^{-2x}\).

SOLUTION \(a=2\): \(F_c=\dfrac{2}{4+s^2}\).

\(\dfrac{2}{s^2+4}\).

Q2

Find the sine transform of \(e^{-3x}\).

SOLUTION \(a=3\): \(F_s=\dfrac{s}{9+s^2}\).

\(\dfrac{s}{s^2+9}\).

Q3

Which transform (sine or cosine) suits an even function, and why?

SOLUTION Cosine is even; pairing an even function with the cosine transform keeps the integrand even and the algebra clean.

Cosine transform.

Unit III

Z-Transforms

The Laplace transform's discrete cousin — for sequences and difference equations, the maths of digital signals.

3.1 The discrete cousin3.2 Standard Z-transforms3.3 Difference equations3.4 Practice
Lesson 3.1

The discrete cousin

In plain words

Where Laplace handles continuous functions \(f(t)\), the Z-transform handles sequences \(y_0,y_1,y_2,\dots\) — numbers sampled at steps, exactly like a digital signal. It turns a difference equation (a recurrence) into algebra in a variable \(z\).

Lesson 3.2

Standard Z-transforms

The table

\(Z\{1\}=\dfrac{z}{z-1}\)    \(Z\{a^n\}=\dfrac{z}{z-a}\)    \(Z\{n\}=\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}\)

Shift rule: \(Z\{y_{n+1}\}=zY(z)-z\,y_0\) — the discrete version of the Laplace derivative rule.

Lesson 3.3

Solving a difference equation

Worked example 3.3a full problem

Solve \(y_{n+1}-2y_n=0\) with \(y_0=3\).

Transform Using the shift rule: \(\big(zY-z\,y_0\big)-2Y=0\Rightarrow (z-2)Y=z\,y_0=3z\).

Solve for Y \(Y(z)=\dfrac{3z}{z-2}\).

Invert Compare with \(Z\{a^n\}=\dfrac{z}{z-a}\): here \(a=2\), and there's a factor 3. So \(y_n=3\cdot 2^n\).

Check \(y_0=3\) ✓; \(y_1=6=2y_0\) ✓.

Unit III don't-lose-marks list
  • The shift rule introduces the initial term \(y_0\) — the discrete analogue of \(-f(0)\).
  • Keep the \(z\) in the numerator when inverting: \(\tfrac{z}{z-a}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{z-a}\) invert differently.

Practice — Unit III

Transform, solve for \(Y(z)\), match the table, invert.

Q1

Find \(Z\{3^n\}\).

SOLUTION \(a=3\).

\(\dfrac{z}{z-3}\).

Q2

Solve \(y_{n+1}-3y_n=0\), \(y_0=1\).

SOLUTION \((z-3)Y=z\Rightarrow Y=\dfrac{z}{z-3}\).

\(y_n=3^n\).

Q3

Solve \(y_{n+1}-y_n=0\), \(y_0=5\).

SOLUTION \((z-1)Y=5z\Rightarrow Y=\dfrac{5z}{z-1}\), so \(y_n=5\cdot 1^n\).

\(y_n=5\) (constant).

Unit IV

Numerical Methods

When no clean formula exists, you compute the answer by patient iteration. A toolbox of reliable recipes.

4.1 Why numerical methods4.2 Newton–Raphson4.3 Gauss–Seidel4.4 Interpolation4.5 ODEs: Euler & RK44.6 Practice
Lesson 4.1

Why numerical methods

In plain words

Many equations can't be solved by a neat formula — for example, \(\cos x = x\). Numerical methods find an answer to any desired accuracy by starting with a guess and improving it step by step.

Lesson 4.2

Newton–Raphson (finding roots)

The idea

Start near a root of \(f(x)=0\). Follow the tangent line down to where it crosses the \(x\)-axis — that's a much better guess. Repeat, and it homes in fast.

Newton–Raphson formula \[x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\]

Worked example 4.2a find a root

Find \(\sqrt2\) by solving \(f(x)=x^2-2=0\), starting at \(x_0=1.5\).

Simplify the formula \(f'(x)=2x\), so \(x_{n+1}=x_n-\dfrac{x_n^2-2}{2x_n}=\dfrac{x_n}{2}+\dfrac{1}{x_n}\).

Step 1 \(x_1=\dfrac{1.5}{2}+\dfrac{1}{1.5}=0.75+0.6667=1.4167\).

Step 2 \(x_2=\dfrac{1.4167}{2}+\dfrac{1}{1.4167}=0.7083+0.7059=1.4142\).

Conclusion Converged to \(\sqrt2\approx1.4142\) in just two steps.

Lesson 4.3

Gauss–Seidel (solving linear systems)

The idea

To solve several equations in several unknowns, rearrange each equation to isolate one variable, then loop through them repeatedly, always using the newest values you have. It converges when the system is "diagonally dominant" (the diagonal coefficient is the biggest in its row).

Worked example 4.3a one round

Solve \(10x+y=12,\ x+10y=21\) by Gauss–Seidel, starting \(y=0\).

Rearrange \(x=\dfrac{12-y}{10}\), \(y=\dfrac{21-x}{10}\).

Iteration 1 \(x=\dfrac{12-0}{10}=1.2\); then \(y=\dfrac{21-1.2}{10}=1.98\).

Iteration 2 \(x=\dfrac{12-1.98}{10}=1.002\); then \(y=\dfrac{21-1.002}{10}=1.9998\).

Conclusion Converging to \(x=1,\ y=2\).

Lesson 4.4

Interpolation (Newton's forward formula)

The idea

Given a table of \((x,y)\) values, interpolation estimates \(y\) at an \(x\) between the tabulated points. Newton's forward formula uses "differences" between successive \(y\)-values.

Newton's forward formula \[y=y_0+p\,\Delta y_0+\frac{p(p-1)}{2!}\Delta^2 y_0+\cdots,\qquad p=\frac{x-x_0}{h}\]

Worked example 4.4a estimate a value

From the table \(x:1,2,3,4\) with \(y=x^2:1,4,9,16\), estimate \(y\) at \(x=1.5\).

Differences \(\Delta y:\ 3,5,7\); \(\Delta^2 y:\ 2,2\); \(\Delta^3 y:\ 0\).

Compute p \(h=1,\ p=\dfrac{1.5-1}{1}=0.5\).

Apply \(y=1+0.5(3)+\dfrac{0.5(0.5-1)}{2}(2)=1+1.5+(-0.125)(2)=1+1.5-0.25=2.25\).

Check \(1.5^2=2.25\) ✓.

Lesson 4.5

Solving ODEs numerically: Euler and Runge–Kutta

The idea

To step a differential equation \(y'=f(x,y)\) forward from a known start, Euler's method takes small straight-line steps using the current slope. Runge–Kutta (RK4) is a more accurate version that averages four slope estimates per step — it's the standard workhorse.

Euler's method \[y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(x_n,y_n)\]
Unit IV don't-lose-marks list
  • Newton–Raphson can misbehave if \(f'(x)\approx0\) or the start is poor — pick a sensible \(x_0\).
  • Gauss–Seidel needs diagonal dominance; rearrange rows if necessary.
  • In interpolation, use forward differences near the top of the table, backward near the bottom.

Practice — Unit IV

Set out iterations neatly in a small table — it prevents arithmetic slips.

Q1

One Newton–Raphson step for \(f(x)=x^2-5\) from \(x_0=2\).

SOLUTION \(x_1=\dfrac{x_0}{2}+\dfrac{5}{2x_0}=1+1.25=2.25\).

\(x_1=2.25\) (true \(\sqrt5\approx2.236\)).

Q2

One Euler step for \(y'=x+y\), \(y(0)=1\), with \(h=0.1\).

SOLUTION \(f(0,1)=0+1=1\). \(y_1=1+0.1(1)=1.1\).

\(y(0.1)\approx1.1\).

Q3

From \(x:0,1,2\), \(y:1,3,7\), find the first and second forward differences.

SOLUTION \(\Delta y:\ 2,4\); \(\Delta^2 y:\ 2\).

\(\Delta y=2,4\); \(\Delta^2 y=2\).

Unit V

Curve Fitting, Correlation & Regression

Turning scattered data into a line and a number: the everyday statistics of analysing experimental results.

5.1 Least-squares line5.2 Correlation5.3 Regression & Spearman5.4 Practice
Lesson 5.1

Fitting a straight line by least squares

The idea

Given data points that roughly follow a line, least squares finds the single line \(y=a+bx\) that sits closest to all of them (minimising the total squared gap). You get \(a\) and \(b\) by solving two "normal equations".

Normal equations for \(y=a+bx\) \[\sum y=na+b\sum x,\qquad \sum xy=a\sum x+b\sum x^2\]

Worked example 5.1a fit a line

Fit a line to \((1,1),(2,2),(3,2),(4,3)\).

Sums \(n=4,\ \sum x=10,\ \sum y=8,\ \sum xy=1+4+6+12=23,\ \sum x^2=30\).

Normal equations \(8=4a+10b\) and \(23=10a+30b\).

Solve From the first, \(a=2-2.5b\). Substitute: \(23=10(2-2.5b)+30b=20+5b\Rightarrow b=0.6\), then \(a=0.5\).

Line \(y=0.5+0.6x\).

Lesson 5.2

Correlation — how tightly linked are two variables?

The idea

Correlation measures how closely two variables move together, as a number \(r\) between \(-1\) and \(+1\). Near \(+1\): they rise together. Near \(-1\): one rises as the other falls. Near \(0\): little linear link.

Karl Pearson's coefficient \[r=\frac{\sum(x-\bar x)(y-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum(x-\bar x)^2\;\sum(y-\bar y)^2}},\qquad -1\le r\le 1\]
Lesson 5.3

Regression lines and Spearman's rank correlation

The idea

There are two regression lines — one to predict \(y\) from \(x\), another to predict \(x\) from \(y\); use whichever matches the prediction asked. When data is ranked (1st, 2nd, 3rd…) rather than measured, use Spearman's rank correlation.

Spearman's rank correlation \[\rho=1-\frac{6\sum d^2}{n(n^2-1)}\]

where \(d\) is the difference between the two ranks of each item.

Worked example 5.3a rank correlation

Four items have rank-differences giving \(\sum d^2=2\), with \(n=4\). Find \(\rho\).

Apply \(\rho=1-\dfrac{6(2)}{4(16-1)}=1-\dfrac{12}{60}=1-0.2\).

Interpret \(\rho=0.8\) — a strong positive agreement between the two rankings.

Unit V don't-lose-marks list
  • The two regression lines are different — use the correct one for the prediction direction.
  • \(r\) must lie in \([-1,1]\); a value outside means an arithmetic error.
  • Correlation is not causation — a high \(r\) doesn't prove one variable causes the other.

Practice — Unit V

Build the sums table (\(\sum x,\sum y,\sum xy,\sum x^2\)) first; everything flows from it.

Q1

For \((1,2),(2,4),(3,6)\), what line fits perfectly?

SOLUTION The points lie exactly on \(y=2x\) (so \(a=0,\ b=2\)).

\(y=2x\).

Q2

Compute Spearman's \(\rho\) if \(\sum d^2=6\), \(n=5\).

SOLUTION \(\rho=1-\dfrac{6(6)}{5(25-1)}=1-\dfrac{36}{120}=1-0.3\).

\(\rho=0.7\).

Q3

What is the range of any correlation coefficient, and what does \(r=0\) mean?

SOLUTION Always \(-1\le r\le1\). \(r=0\) means no linear relationship between the variables.

\([-1,1]\); \(r=0\Rightarrow\) no linear link.

Your weekly routine
  • Read one unit's lessons, working every example by hand.
  • Practise that unit with solutions hidden; re-do only the ones you missed.
  • Build a one-page sheet per unit — the Laplace/Z tables, the Newton–Raphson formula, the normal equations.
  • Finish with matching questions from an Osmania University past paper.

About this page

A beginner-friendly, worked tutorial for Engineering Mathematics-IV (BS 401 MT), BE Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering (Autonomous), Osmania University. Explanations, examples and practice are standard treatments aligned to the prescribed texts (Grewal; Jain & Iyengar; Vasishtha & Gupta; Kreyszig; Gupta & Kapoor). Equations render with MathJax; tap "Show solution" to check your practice answers.

Note: the UCE Mathematics department publishes a single Mathematics-IV paper (BS 401 MT). BME follows this standard fourth mathematics course — confirm against your printed B.E. (BME) IV-semester scheme in case of a branch-specific revision.

Self-contained single file — upload index.html to tiiny.host to publish. Needs an internet connection to load web fonts and the MathJax renderer.  Compiled study reference, not an official university publication.  Prepared 04 July 2026.
